title: How Claude Code changed my work as a Data Scientist
subtitle: One data scientist's before-and-after, in the numbers
blocks: Companies write big checks for coding-agent subscriptions, and now they're asking about ROI. Most of that debate is about engineers. Here's what it looks like from a data scientist's perspective.

blocks.orig: I did what an analyst does — took the data on how I work, analyzed it, and put it in this post as a snapshot.
blocks.tr: I analyzed the data on my own work and wrote it up here.
blocks.g: Two clauses joined by te-form: 分析して … 書きました ("analyzed, and then wrote").

blocks: I joined Finom as the lead DS focused on AI evals — building evaluation company-wide from scratch and lifting quality in our AI products. After leaving Yandex, where I led a large analytics team, I was curious how far agentic coding could take one person.

jp: データサイエンティスト
blocks.orig: I was curious how far one DS can go with agentic coding.
blocks.tr: I wanted to know how far a single data scientist could go.
blocks.g: Embedded question with か: どこまでできるか ("how far one can go") inside 知りたかった.

blocks: I analyzed 9 months of my Jira, Git and Claude Code history and compared Q4'25 with Q2'26.
Q4'25: I did analysis by hand, copy-pasted snippets from ChatGPT, and used Cursor for some tasks (though, honestly, I didn't like it much).

blocks.orig: I installed Claude Code, tried Opus 4.5, and it clicked!
blocks.tr: I installed Claude Code, and when I tried Opus 4.5, I liked it right away.
blocks.g: 〜てみる (try doing) + 〜たら (when): 使ってみたら = "when I gave it a try."

blocks: Q2'26: I built a personal harness (context, tools, access, skills) so that every task is powered by Claude Code.
1. The analytics job itself (Python scripts, SQL, eval datasets, prompts) grew ×4.6, from 12k to 58k lines per quarter.

jp: できるようになりました
came to be able to do
blocks.orig: The same job I always did, now more and faster.
blocks.tr: It's the same job, but I've come to be able to do more of it, and faster.
blocks.g: New point 〜ようになる: attach to a plain verb to mean "reach a state of doing / come to do." できる→できるようになる = "came to be able to."

blocks: (Yes, lines of code are a poor productivity metric — but scroll down for the business impact.)
2. And I stopped being only an analyst. Developing the eval harness, making changes in production code, quality-affecting fixes — I now write them myself.

blocks.orig: Quality-affecting fixes I used to hand off to devs — now I write them myself.
blocks.tr: Fixes I used to ask developers for, I now write myself.
blocks.g: 〜ていた for a past habit: 頼んでいた = "used to ask (repeatedly)."

blocks: The share of tasks I ship directly vs. hand to developers: 28% in Q4'25 → 93% in Q2'26. It flipped!
Production changes: ~0.1k → 4.0k lines across ~50 commits in Q2'26.
Repos I touch: 1 → 8, including backend and MCP services.

blocks.orig: The wall between "analytics code" and "service code" is gone.
blocks.tr: The wall between "analytics code" and "service code" has disappeared.
blocks.g: なくなる = "become non-existent / disappear" (ない + なる).

blocks: Before Claude Code I shipped almost zero code to our services; now I do part of the engineering myself.
Put those together and the analysis got bigger and better, while time-to-market went from days to hours.

blocks.orig: There's a step between "the analysis says X" and "X is live" — the handoff.
blocks.tr: The step between "analysis" and "production" is called a "handoff."
blocks.g: New point: passive 〜られる. 呼ぶ (to call) → 呼ばれる (is called); the thing is X と呼ばれる = "is called X."

blocks: That handoff — between "the analysis says X" and "X is live" — is what disappeared.

blocks.orig: Time-to-market went from days to hours — because there's no longer a handoff.
blocks.tr: Because the handoff is gone, the time shrank from days to hours.
blocks.g: 〜ので (because): なくなったので = "because it disappeared."

blocks.orig: I'm honestly amazed at how much this has changed my job.
blocks.tr: Honestly, my job changed this much, and I'm very amazed.
blocks.g: Te-form as cause: 変わって、驚いています = "it changed, so I'm amazed."

blocks.orig: And it still feels like just the beginning.
blocks.tr: But I think this is still just the beginning.
blocks.g: 〜と思う (I think): plain clause + と + 思います.

blocks: I'm curious to see where deeper integration, loop engineering, and better models will take us.

blocks.orig: Let's see how far this goes!
blocks.tr: I'm looking forward to seeing how far this goes.
blocks.g: Embedded question 〜か + 楽しみ: どこまで進むか、楽しみ = "looking forward to how far it goes."
